content.text: Recurring Incident Prevention Tracking

A single incident is a failure. The same incident happening twice is a process failure. The same incident happening three times is a leadership failure. Recurring incident prevention tracking is the practice of detecting when the same root cause is producing repeated failures, escalating it to a systemic fix track, and measuring whether preventive actions are working.
The Recurrence Problem in AI Systems

AI platform incidents are particularly prone to recurrence because their root causes are often probabilistic or load-dependent, not deterministic bugs. A model that occasionally produces malformed output under specific token patterns will keep failing until the underlying issue is addressed at the model or validation layer — partial fixes, like increasing retries, only reduce frequency without eliminating the cause.
Common recurring patterns in AI production systems:
Token budget exhaustion
under peak load, triggering cascading timeouts across dependent services
Embedding distribution drift
causing retrieval quality to degrade silently until a downstream metric crosses a threshold
Rate limit collisions
between multiple services sharing an upstream LLM API without coordinated backoff
Safety filter bypasses
via adversarial prompt patterns that were not covered in the evaluation suite
Stale feature cache
serving predictions computed on outdated context after a model version rollout
Each of these recurs because the first fix addressed the symptom (the specific failure instance) rather than the systemic cause (the architectural vulnerability that allows that class of failure).
Defining Recurrence

Two incidents share a root cause if they required the same underlying architectural or operational change to permanently prevent. This is a tighter definition than 'same component failed' or 'same alert fired'. An alert for high inference latency can fire for a dozen different root causes. A recurrence is when the postmortem for incident B identifies the same contributing factor as incident A.
Operationally, detect recurrence by tagging postmortems with structured root cause labels:

# Postmortem root cause taxonomy (enforce via PR template or issue label set)
root_cause_categories:
  - capacity: [gpu-exhaustion, cpu-saturation, memory-oom, token-budget-exceeded]
  - model: [drift, hallucination-spike, safety-bypass, eval-gap, stale-checkpoint]
  - integration: [rate-limit, api-timeout, schema-mismatch, version-skew]
  - infrastructure: [network-partition, storage-failure, dns-resolution, tls-expiry]
  - process: [deploy-without-rollback, missing-runbook, wrong-oncall-owner, alert-not-configured]

# Each postmortem gets 1-3 labels from this taxonomy
# Recurrence detection: query for the same leaf label in 2+ incidents within rolling 90 days
Without structured labels, recurrence detection requires humans to remember and manually link incidents — which is unreliable. With labels, it becomes a database query.
Recurrence Thresholds by Severity
Different severity levels warrant different recurrence tolerances. A SEV-1 recurring once is already an emergency. A SEV-3 pattern might only justify systemic action after multiple occurrences.
Severity
Recurrence Threshold
Action Required
Escalation Level
SEV-1
2nd incident same root cause
Immediate systemic fix initiative; deployment gate
VP Engineering + Safety
SEV-2
3rd incident same root cause
Systemic fix in next sprint; P0 priority
Engineering Director
SEV-3
5th incident same root cause
Architectural review in quarterly planning
Engineering Manager

When a threshold is crossed, the individual action items from each incident are superseded by a single systemic fix initiative. Treating the sixth occurrence of the same SEV-3 root cause as six independent action items is a governance failure — it produces six owners working in parallel on overlapping solutions.
The Systemic Fix Initiative
A systemic fix initiative is a dedicated project that replaces all individual action items tracking the same root cause. It has:
A single senior owner
(typically Staff Eng or above) with authority to make architectural decisions
A 30-day target
for SEV-1/2 patterns; 90-day target for SEV-3 patterns
A definition of done
that includes both the fix and a mechanism that prevents recurrence (automated test, deployment gate, runbook)
VP-level visibility
— reported in the weekly engineering review until closed
Initiative Closure Criteria
A systemic fix initiative is not complete when the code change is merged. It is complete when:
The fix is deployed and verified in production
A regression test or automated check is in place that would have caught the original incident
All prior postmortems that contributed to this initiative are updated with a link to the systemic fix
The recurrence rate metric (see below) has been flat or declining for 14 days post-deployment
The Recurrence Rate Metric
Aggregate recurrence into a single metric that the engineering team reviews monthly:

def recurrence_rate(incidents, window_days=90):
    """
    Fraction of incidents in the window whose root-cause tag
    appeared in a prior incident within the same window.
    """
    from datetime import timedelta

    incidents = sorted(incidents, key=lambda i: i["date"])
    window_start = incidents[-1]["date"] - timedelta(days=window_days)
    window = [i for i in incidents if i["date"] >= window_start]

    seen_causes = set()
    recurring = 0

    for inc in window:
        causes = set(inc.get("root_cause_labels", []))
        if causes & seen_causes:   # intersection: at least one cause seen before
            recurring += 1
        seen_causes |= causes

    return recurring / len(window) if window else 0.0

# Example usage
rate = recurrence_rate(all_incidents)
print(f"Recurrence rate (90d): {rate:.1%}")
# Target: < 10%
# Warning: 10-25%
# Red flag: > 25%

A recurrence rate above 25% means more than one in four incidents is a repeat of a known failure mode — which indicates either postmortem action items are not being completed, or they are being completed in ways that do not eliminate the root cause.
Prevention Categories and Selection

When designing a systemic fix, choose the prevention category that eliminates the highest amount of future risk per unit of engineering effort. The five categories, ordered from least to most durable:
1. Detect Earlier (MTTD reduction)
Add alerting, dashboards, or anomaly detection that surfaces the failure mode before it reaches customer impact. This does not prevent the failure, but reduces its blast radius.
Best for: failure modes that are genuinely difficult or expensive to eliminate (e.g., model drift is a statistical property of the data distribution, not a bug that can be patched).

# Example: add p95 latency alert for inference endpoint
# Fires before cascading timeout hits downstream services
alert_config = {
    "name": "inference_latency_high",
    "condition": "p95(inference_duration_seconds) > 8.0",
    "window": "5m",
    "severity": "SEV-2",
    "runbook": "https://docs.internal/runbooks/inference-latency",
}
2. Reduce Blast Radius
Add circuit breakers, rate limits, bulkheads, or graceful degradation paths so that when the failure occurs, it affects fewer users or services.

Best for: integration failures where one dependent service can cause a cascade (e.g., LLM API rate limit exhaustion causing all inference requests to fail instead of queuing and degrading gracefully).
3. Eliminate Root Cause
Fix the architectural vulnerability that allows the failure class to occur. This is the most durable prevention but often the most expensive.

Best for: recurring failures in components the team owns directly. If token budget exhaustion keeps occurring because all services share one API key with no coordination, the fix is a token budget management layer, not more retries.
4. Automate Recovery
Build self-healing: automated rollback triggers, auto-scaling responses, or runbook automation that resolves the failure without human intervention.
Best for: failure modes that will still occur occasionally (e.g., a GPU node going down under load) but where the recovery steps are deterministic and fast enough to automate.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Auto-rollback trigger: fires when error rate exceeds threshold post-deploy
set -euo pipefail

DEPLOY_ID=$1
ERROR_RATE=$(curl -s "${METRICS_URL}/error_rate?window=5m" | jq '.value')

if (( $(echo "$ERROR_RATE > 0.05" | bc -l) )); then
  echo "Error rate ${ERROR_RATE} exceeds 5% threshold -- initiating rollback"
  kubectl rollout undo deployment/ai-inference-service
  post_incident_alert "Auto-rollback triggered for deploy ${DEPLOY_ID}"
fi
5. Tolerate Failure
Design the system to absorb the failure class without customer impact through redundancy, multi-region failover, or fallback model paths.
Best for: infrastructure failures that cannot be eliminated (hardware failure, network partitions) and where downtime cost exceeds the cost of redundancy.
Tracking Prevention Effectiveness
Completing a systemic fix initiative is not the end of the tracking cycle. Measure whether the fix actually reduced recurrence:
Metric
How to measure
When to escalate
Post-fix recurrence
Any incident with same root-cause label after fix deployed
Immediately — means fix was incomplete
MTTD improvement
Avg time from failure start to alert fire, before vs after
No improvement after 30 days
Blast radius reduction
Affected-user-hours per incident, before vs after
No reduction after 30 days
Recurrence rate trend
90-day rolling rate, measured monthly
Still rising 60 days post-fix

If a systemic fix initiative is closed and the root cause recurs within 90 days, the initiative is reopened — not a new initiative created. This prevents the 'we already fixed that' confusion where a second initiative duplicates work without reviewing why the first failed.
Integrating Recurrence Tracking into the Engineering Review
Recurrence prevention tracking only works if it is visible to the people who allocate engineering capacity. Add a standing section to the monthly engineering review:

Monthly Engineering Review -- Reliability Section (10 minutes)

1. Recurrence rate (90-day rolling): [X%]  Target: < 10%
2. Active systemic fix initiatives: [N]
   - [initiative name] | Owner: [name] | ETA: [date] | Status: [color]
3. Newly crossed recurrence thresholds this month:
   - [root-cause-label] crossed SEV-2 threshold (3 incidents) -- initiating fix
4. Initiatives closed last month + verification status:
   - [initiative name] closed [date] -- recurrence rate flat for [N] days
This format takes 10 minutes and gives leadership the signal they need to allocate reliability capacity before the recurrence rate climbs further.
Preventing Recurrence Through Chaos Engineering

The most reliable way to confirm that a systemic fix is durable is to deliberately trigger the failure mode in a controlled environment before it recurs in production. For recurring AI platform failures, design targeted chaos experiments:
Chaos Experiment Design for Known Failure Patterns

Each systemic fix initiative should produce a companion chaos experiment that exercises the fixed failure mode. The experiment documents what the system should do (degrade gracefully, alert, auto-recover) versus what the old system did (cascade failure, silent data corruption, silent timeout).

If the chaos experiment fails (system behaves the same as before the fix), the systemic fix initiative is reopened. The chaos experiment becomes the regression test that prevents the pattern from silently re-emerging as the codebase evolves.
Game Days for Complex Recurring Patterns

For failure patterns that are difficult to reproduce with code injection (e.g., model safety bypasses, data pipeline corruption), run a game day: a scheduled exercise where the on-call team works through the failure scenario using a staging environment with injected bad data or adversarial prompts. Game days:
Verify that runbooks are accurate and the fix is understood by the team, not just the engineer who wrote it
Surface procedural gaps (e.g., the runbook says 'contact the ML platform team' but that team's on-call rotation is not in PagerDuty)
Build muscle memory so the next real incident is resolved faster
Cross-Team Recurrence Patterns

Some recurring failures are not owned by a single team — they emerge from the interaction between two or more services. These are the hardest to prevent because no single team has the authority to mandate the systemic fix across the entire dependency chain.
Identifying Cross-Team Patterns
A failure is cross-team when the postmortem identifies contributing factors in two or more separate services or codebases. Tag these postmortems with a 'cross-team' label and the teams involved:

# Postmortem labels for cross-team recurring pattern
labels:
  - postmortem-action
  - root-cause:rate-limit
  - teams:ai-serving,ml-platform,api-gateway
  - cross-team: true

# Recurrence detection query: same root-cause + cross-team + overlapping team set
# signals a systemic integration gap, not a single-team bug
The Joint Fix Initiative
Cross-team recurrence patterns require a joint systemic fix initiative with representation from all involved teams. Key differences from a single-team initiative:
The initiative owner is typically a Staff or Principal Engineer with cross-team authority, designated by the engineering director
The initiative has a joint closure review: all contributing teams must sign off that their part of the fix is complete and verified
The chaos experiment covers the integration boundary, not just each team's internal behaviour

A cross-team systemic fix that is closed by one team but not the other is not closed. The recurrence tracking system should require sign-off from all teams listed in the initiative before moving it to 'closed' state.
Special Case: AI Safety Recurrences

Any recurrence involving a safety-relevant root cause (content policy bypass, harmful output at scale, RLHF reward hacking) is treated as SEV-1 regardless of the severity of the original incidents. A safety bypass pattern that manifested as SEV-2 three times is a SEV-1 systemic problem.

Safety recurrences trigger an additional process step: a formal safety review meeting that includes the AI safety team, not just engineering. The meeting produces a threat model update (what variants of this bypass pattern also need to be tested?) and a deployment gate that is not lifted until the systemic fix passes a dedicated red-team evaluation.

The test for whether a systemic fix is complete is not 'does the fix address what we observed?' but 'does the fix address the class of failure, including variants we have not yet observed?' For safety-relevant patterns, the second question is the only one that matters.
Summary

Recurring incident prevention tracking converts the observation that 'this has happened before' into a structured engineering response. The core components are structured root-cause tagging on postmortems, automated recurrence detection against severity-calibrated thresholds, systemic fix initiatives that replace scattered individual action items, and a recurrence rate metric that makes prevention effectiveness visible. The five prevention categories — detect earlier, reduce blast radius, eliminate root cause, automate recovery, tolerate failure — provide a structured framework for choosing fixes that are durable rather than tactical. Safety recurrences are treated as SEV-1 regardless of original severity and require a full safety review and red-team evaluation before the systemic fix is considered complete.
Key takeaways:
Tag every postmortem with structured root-cause labels to enable automated recurrence detection
SEV-1 recurs twice: immediate systemic fix + deployment gate. SEV-2 recurs three times: next-sprint P0
Systemic fix initiatives replace all individual action items tracking the same root cause
Recurrence rate (target < 10%) is the primary metric for prevention effectiveness
Safety pattern recurrences are always SEV-1 and require red-team validation before closure
